subject: mathematics

core_competencies.justification: Sections 1.1–1.4 develop sense for large whole numbers by reading, writing, naming (Indian and international systems), comparing, and estimating (rounding to nearest thousand through crore) numbers up to 9–10 digits; sections 1.5–1.6 apply arithmetic operations on large numbers throughout.

Section 1.5 explicitly discovers and describes rules for number patterns — the digit-count boundary theorem (product of two d-digit numbers is (2d-1)- or 2d-digit) proved through boundary-case reasoning, and multiplication pattern tables (11×11, 111×111…) extended by students — forming a structurally distinct strand from the naming/operations work of C-1.1.

adjunct_competencies.justification: Section 1.5 requires students to justify multiplication shortcuts (why ×5 = ÷2 then ×10) using the meaning of operations — a deductive argument — and to prove the digit-count boundary theorem via boundary reasoning, moving beyond calculation into mathematical proof.

Section 1.6 engages systematic counting and reasoning about large quantities (population in buses, distance to Moon/Sun, digit-sequence problems E-46) requiring students to devise and follow iterative estimation algorithms, not just compute.

Section 1.6's open-ended puzzles (digit-rearrangement for largest multiples of 5, letters in number names, strike-out digit problems) invite students to discover their own creative solution strategies rather than follow prescribed procedures.

dissolution_test: This chapter builds the student's ability to name, read, and operate on large whole numbers by extending the base-ten place value system and discovering structural patterns in number magnitude and products.
